exercises.title: An A2A Agent Card for discovery
exercises.prompt: A2A discovery works through an Agent Card: a JSON document advertising an agent's identity, skills, and endpoint. Write a TypeScript `buildAgentCard(agent)` that produces a well-formed card with name, description, url, and a skills array (each skill has id, name, and inputModes/outputModes), and a `findSkill(card, capability)` that searches a card for an agent that can do a given capability. Build a card for a 'translator' agent and look up 'translate'.
exercises.hint: An Agent Card is { name, description, url, skills: [{ id, name, inputModes, outputModes }] }. findSkill scans card.skills for one whose name/id matches the requested capability. This is how one agent decides whether another can do what it needs before delegating.

exercises.title: MCP tool discovery and invocation
exercises.prompt: MCP is the *vertical* protocol: agent ↔ tools. An MCP server advertises tools (list), and the agent invokes one by name with arguments (call). Build a minimal MCP-style server in TypeScript with `listTools()` returning tool schemas and `callTool(name, args)` dispatching to the implementation, plus an agent-side `useTool(server, name, args)` that validates the call against the advertised schema before invoking. Show a valid call succeeding and an invalid one (missing required arg) being rejected before invocation.
exercises.hint: Server: tools map name -> {schema: {required: string[]}, impl: fn}. listTools returns names+schemas. callTool runs impl. Agent-side useTool: fetch schema, check all required args present, only then call. Reject early if a required arg is missing — schema validation is the safety boundary.

exercises.title: Route a task across MCP, A2A, ACP, and ANP
exercises.prompt: Four protocols solve four problems: MCP (agent↔tools), A2A (agent↔agent), ACP (enterprise audit/compliance), ANP (decentralized trust). Build a `routeProtocol(request)` that, given the nature of a request, selects the correct protocol and explains why — and flag requests that need *two* protocols composed (the common production case of MCP+A2A). Run it on four requests and discuss why production systems pair protocols rather than picking one.
exercises.hint: Signals: needs a tool -> MCP; needs another agent -> A2A; needs audit trail / regulated -> ACP; needs trust across untrusted orgs -> ANP. A request can trigger several (e.g. 'have another agent that uses a tool' -> A2A + MCP). Return the set of protocols + reasoning.
